data.title: Zions Bancorporation: Solid Q1 Results
data.description:
We are early in our coverage of the Q1 regional bank earnings season. We believe reviewing the earnings of regional banks is a great way to do what we call a "pulse check" on the strength of the economy both locally and regionally. Thus far, we have seen overall stability across the sector in the number of reports we have reviewed. It has been a good sample thus far of large and smaller banks, and thus far we just aren’t seeing any evidence of a slowdown in borrowing or ability of borrowers to pay their loans. We thought we might see this because of the macro concerns in March with the war and gas prices surging. But so far, it is not reflected in the data we have checked so far.

In today's column, we turn our attention to Zions Bancorporation, National Association (ZION). This one is based out of Salt Lake City and operating across 11 Western states. So this bank gives us another sample of data to help better understand the economic health of the Mountain West and Pacific regions. After reviewing the just-reported Q1 results, we see a bank with good earnings growth and solid credit metrics. Let us check in on and discuss the key metrics we follow.

Zions Bancorporation Q1 Earnings Headline Results

In the just-reported Q1, Zions delivered a strong performance on the top and bottom lines, in our opinion. Total revenue hit $849 million, up 7.4% year-over-year. The provision for credit losses was notable as it was a $7 million recovery versus an actual $18 million a year ago. This helped boost earnings, and this is because the provisions directly impact earnings. Net income was $232 million, or $1.56 per share. Folks, this represents a massive 38% increase from the $1.13 reported a year ago. It's also up from $1.48 in the sequential quarter. It beat estimates by $0.13.

Zions Financial Q1 Loans And Deposits

We always look into the trends in loans and deposits when we do a bank earnings review. And for both of these key metrics, trends are moving in the right direction. Loans grew to $61.3 billion, a 2% increase from the prior year. This growth was driven by an $860 million increase in commercial loans and a $458 million increase in consumer loans, particularly in home equity lines. We also saw total deposits up to $76.9 billion. This was stable growth of 2% year-over-year. As you can see, the company has a long runway of deposits to lend against. Overall these trends were good in our opinion.

Zions Financial Q1 Asset Quality And Return Metrics

Asset quality at Zions is among the cleanest we have seen this season, even though it's early in our coverage of Q1 results. But it was really good. The annualized ratio of net loan charge-offs to average loans was a mere 0.03%, down from 0.11% a year ago. Further, the so-called classified loans that are at risk dropped by 19% over the past year to $2.3 billion. A lot of this was due to reductions in commercial real estate exposures. Also, nonperforming assets were down to $292 million. This was down to 0.48% of total loans, a decrease from the 0.51% reported last year.

Margins were up nicely, too. The net interest margin came in at 3.27%, up from 3.10% a year ago, though it did edge down slightly from the sequential Q4. The improvement was due to lower funding costs as rates have come down, while yields on loans have held up. If there is one gripe we have with the bank's performance, it is that it is not as efficient as many others. It is average at best. The good news is that the bank’s efficiency ratio improved to 65.0% from 66.6% last year, but the best banks we cover from this standpoint are under 50% on this metric.

Final Thoughts On Zions

Zions Bancorporation’s Q1 report adds to the evidence base thus far that despite all of the fear over the economy and the pain of gas and utility prices, there are no signs of a slowdown in borrowing or asset quality. We are seeing a return to growth in both loans and customer deposits, paired with some of the best credit metrics we have seen so far. While it is not as efficient as other banks, the company is a quality operator. There are repurchases here and, of course, a dividend, which is nice to have when owning any stock. We would like to be buyers a little closer to book value, which was up to $41.75, so at this level we rate it a Hold.

data.title: Kohl's: As Long As The Demand Is Weak I Would Give It A Pass
data.description:
Both in the most recent quarter and in fiscal 2025, Kohl's (KSS) showed many signs of weakness. Net sales and comparable sales declined meaningfully. The guidance they issued also does not give much hope for optimism in the near term, especially considering the level of consumer sentiment and the elevated energy prices. While the company's 3.4% dividend yield may appear attractive at first, the inconsistency of the payments and of the growth make it an unattractive dividend stock as well. For these reasons, I believe it is not worth owning KSS right now.

Financials

In the most recent quarter, KSS posted a net sales decline of 3.9%, as well as a comparable sales decline of 2.8%. While the decline by itself is already unattractive, the fact that the decline is broad-based makes these figures look even worse.

All but one segment showed a decline in net sales in 2025 compared to 2024. It means that it is not a single category that drives the poor performance, but in fact most of them, indicating that the demand for discretionary products sold by KSS is weak overall. Transaction volume declined by 4%.

This is no surprise if we take a closer look at the macroeconomic environment, specifically at the consumer confidence levels. Sentiment in 2025 has been around the historic lows, meaning that people were less certain about their financial stability as well as about the overall health of the economy. When sentiment is poor, people are likely to spend less on discretionary products. This is well reflected in the sales results. Looking ahead, I do not expect the sales to jump up significantly. In April 2026, consumer confidence hit record low levels, which, in my view, means that people are still not ready to spend.

I also have to note that this was the third consecutive year of declining comparable sales.

On the other hand, I have to point out that KSS did a great job managing the cost side of the business in the most recent quarter. The gross margin expanded due to less markdowns, while SG&A expenses went down due to lower spending in stores, marketing, and fulfilment.

Looking forward, I am not so optimistic about the margins. Since the outbreak of the war in the Middle East, energy prices are much higher than they were in the past two years. High oil prices generally tend to lead to increased transportation and manufacturing costs. If KSS is not able to pass these elevated expenses over to their customers, their margins are likely to contract. Seeing how the demand has been developing for the company's products, I do not think it will be easy to pass on the expenses without losing customers.

If we take a look at the company's outlook, they expect that net sales and comparable sales either stay flat or decline slightly. I believe that these expectations are realistic, considering the weak consumer sentiment. The expectation for shrinking operating margin is also realistic. And based on this guidance, I believe it is difficult to get excited about KSS in the near term.

Before moving on to the next section, there is one more item that I would like to highlight, and that is debt. The firm has more than $6.6 billion in debt, while their cash on hand is less than $700 million. Such a capital structure is not uncommon for retailers, but I do think it is important to pay attention to, especially as the interest rate environment became much more uncertain. Higher interest rates for longer mean higher interest expenses and more pressure on the bottom line. Leases, both operating and financial, make up a larger part of this debt, though.

Capital Allocation and Dividends

With this, let us move on to the capital allocation of the firm. The firm remains focused on investing into the business and reducing the debt, which are both important. They are also committed to returning value to their shareholders, both through dividends and share buybacks.

The dividend payments have been, however, relatively inconsistent. When the COVID-19 pandemic broke out, KSS paused their dividend, which at that time was the right move - in my view - as the demand for their products significantly dropped. After they resumed the dividend payments, they even managed to increase it slightly before having to cut it again. When investing in a firm for its dividends, I normally prefer more consistent payments than what KSS offers.

When it comes to share buybacks, the firm indicated that they want to resume them in the long term, but I believe that it is not going to happen when the demand and the fundamentals are still weak and while there is significant debt on the balance sheet. 2022 was the last fiscal year when KSS still spent a meaningful amount on buybacks.

While I believe that debt reduction is important, I do not find KSS attractive for income-focused investors due to the uncertainty around dividend growth and share buybacks.

Valuation

Based on a set of traditional price multiples, KSS may appear cheap. And in fact, many retailers appear cheap now, especially compared to their 5-year average metrics. It does not mean, however, that they are undervalued. In my view, as long as the fundamentals are deteriorating - and by that I mean that sales are declining and the profitability remains uncertain due to elevated energy prices - no matter how attractive the valuation looks, it is not worth owning the firm.

At this point, however, I also have to talk about upside potential, as throughout the article my view was quite bearish. First, KSS already demonstrated that it is able to control costs and manage inventory efficiently. If they manage to do so in the coming quarters, their bottom-line results may not be as negatively impacted by the macroeconomic headwinds. Second, if a U.S.-Iran deal were achieved, sentiment could significantly improve, which could cause a rebound not just in the price of KSS, but in general across the market. Last, but not least, we have to mention that the short interest in KSS is very high, as much as 27%, which means a short squeeze could also occur, potentially leading to high volatility and meaningful upside potential. This may be of interest to speculative investors.

Conclusions

Although margins slightly improved in the most recent quarter due to efficient inventory management and lower SG&A expenses, the sales figures remained weak, driven by soft demand. Sales are expected to be flat or trend slightly down in the near future, mainly driven by the macroeconomic headwinds.

The firm's high debt level requires attention, and it is clearly visible from the capital allocation priorities. This, however, makes the firm less attractive for income-focused investors due to the uncertainty related to dividend growth and share buybacks.

Although price multiples seem low, I cannot recommend a firm from a valuation point of view while the fundamentals are deteriorating. There could be an upside potential if a U.S.-Iran deal were reached or if a short squeeze were to happen.

I rate KSS as a sell.

data.title: QXO And TopBuild Tie The Knot But Don't Earn The Upgrade
data.description:
April 20 was a fascinating day for market participants who owned shares of TopBuild Corp. (BLD) and QXO, Inc. (QXO). This is because news broke a day earlier that the latter is acquiring the former in a cash and stock deal worth about $16.89 billion on an enterprise value basis. This is a monumental transaction that will pave the way for additional growth for QXO. And that is saying a lot because the firm has been on a buying spree as of late. It is rapidly transforming into a giant in its space. And moving forward, investors would be wise to expect further expansion.

In the past, I have taken a rather neutral stance on the company. The only time I have written about it was back in March of last year when news broke that it was acquiring Beacon Roofing Supply in a transaction worth $11 billion. And in that article, I called it a "Hold" based on its overall valuation. In my most recent article about TopBuild, which was published just last month, I took the same stance. But since then, shares are up 38.5%, meaningfully outpacing the 11% rise that the S&P 500 saw over the same window of time. This move higher was driven largely by the 18.6% jump that shares experienced on April 20 in response to this development.

Fundamentally speaking, I must say that I am optimistic about the deal. I believe that investors in TopBuild are getting a good price for the business. Even without synergies, there's the opportunity for investors in QXO to walk away from this with a bargain as well. And if synergies are captured, the deal will look very attractive indeed. It's not enough for me to upgrade either firm at this time. But from a purely operational standpoint, I cannot help but be bullish.

Digging Into The Deal

On April 19, after the market closed, news broke that QXO agreed to acquire TopBuild in a cash and stock deal worth $16.89 billion. According to the terms of the agreement, shareholders in TopBuild will have the option of receiving either $505 per share in cash or 20.2 shares of QXO in exchange for each share of TopBuild that they own. There is a limit to this, though. Management has specifically designed the deal to cap out the share component to represent no more than 55% of the value of the transaction. The other 45% will be in cash, which translates to about $6.40 billion that QXO must come up with.

Most of this will be debt. However, management did reveal in their investor presentation regarding the transaction that $1 billion of the purchase price will come from the issuance of additional stock. Specifically, in January, QXO announced that it had struck a deal with funds managed by affiliates of Apollo Global Management (APO) and other miscellaneous unnamed investors that call for a combined investment in preferred units of QXO in the amount of up to $1.2 billion. This would be convertible preferred stock that pays out a rate of 4.75% annually and that has an initial conversion price of $23.25 per share. After seeing its share price drop 4.4% on April 20 in response to the news that it was acquiring TopBuild, QXO’s stock is still trading at $23.71. So technically, those units are still in the green.

Conceptually, this acquisition makes a lot of sense. And it follows some other purchases that the firm made. I already mentioned the $11 billion acquisition of Beacon Roofing Supply that occurred last year. But then, on April 1 of this year, QXO completed its purchase of Kodiak Building Partners, a distributor of lumber, trusses, and other building materials. That cost shareholders $2.25 billion. $2 billion of this was in the form of cash, with the remaining $250 million in the form of 13.2 million shares. Now when we add in TopBuild to the mix, we get a firm that will generate around $6.2 billion in annual revenue that serves as the largest distributor and installer of insulation and related building products in North America. This fits perfectly with QXO, which is the largest publicly traded distributor of roofing, waterproofing, and related products, not to mention the second largest distributor of lumber and building materials, throughout the nation.

Before we get in any deeper, I think that it would be helpful to look at the chart above. In it, you can see financial performance for TopBuild for the last three completed fiscal years. The company has achieved respectable growth during this time. Since QXO itself has a history of being more of a shell company than anything else, I don't think it is terribly useful to provide a breakdown of its historical financial performance. It's worth noting that it generated $6.84 billion in revenue last year. That was up from only $56.9 million reported the previous year. All of this growth came from the acquisition of Beacon Roofing Supply.

Management has some major ambitions in acquiring these various enterprises. The goal of the firm has been explicitly stated as advancing toward a market leader in the building products distribution space for North America. They hope to grow revenue to $50 billion through organic growth and acquisitions. On the organic side of things, TopBuild should certainly help. The business is expected to grow from $5.41 billion in 2025 in terms of revenue to between $9 billion and $10 billion by the year 2030. This would result in EBITDA of between $1.7 billion and $2 billion, which is well above the $1.04 billion the company generated last year. Although a lot of this will also be attributable to acquisition activities that management had outlined. But now, the combined business will have even more market power, potentially making it easier to expand rapidly. Even if we discount the future plans that these businesses have, there is no denying that the industries in which they operate happen to be massive. Combined, you're looking at a market opportunity of $300 billion within the larger $800 billion building products space.

When factoring in all acquisition activities that QXO engaged in leading up to this point, that business has pro forma revenue of $11.9 billion and pro forma EBITDA amounting to $1 billion. Meanwhile, TopBuild will add $6.2 billion of revenue and $1.1 billion of EBITDA to this. The end result will be an enterprise with $18.1 billion of revenue and $2.1 billion of EBITDA. This does not even factor in an expected $300 million in annualized synergies expected to be fully phased in by 2030.

If we use the estimates that management provided, we can see in the chart below just how QXO and TopBuild look on a standalone basis at current pricing. For the latter, I am factoring in the buyout price. You can also see what the combined business should look like from a valuation standpoint, both with synergies and without them. Although QXO is paying a premium on a price-to-sales basis and on an enterprise-value-to-sales basis, it is getting a discount when it comes to profitability. The combined firm looks especially attractive if synergies do come to fruition. But investors should not bank on that since they are not guaranteed. Unfortunately, this alone is not enough to justify a bullish outlook. After all, these are multiples that I consider to be more in the fair value range than I do the undervalued range. However, what the company is working to accomplish is impressive.

Takeaway

I believe that QXO is making a wise move here. The fact that it's using so much of its stock to pay for a business that, in relation to cash flow, is cheaper than it is, is promising. Yes, it's paying a premium in relation to revenue. But at the end of the day, cash flows matter more. In total, I believe that this transaction is a net positive for it while it also serves as a good exit point for investors in TopBuild. But from a valuation standpoint, I don't find either company particularly compelling. Because of this, I believe that both deserve to keep the "Hold" rating I assigned to them previously.

data.title: QGRO Can Deliver Healthy Risk-Adjusted Returns In 2026
data.description:
The American Century US Quality Growth ETF’s (QGRO) share price could soar nearly 20% in 2026, given improving geopolitical and fundamental factors. In the past three years bull run, QGRO’s average annual price return was more than 26%, in line with the spliced U.S. Quality Growth Index but down by 5% from the Russell 1000 Growth Index. The fund’s returns seem impressive due to its strategy of maintaining a portfolio combination of high-growth and stable growers to achieve healthy risk-adjusted returns. I maintain my buy ratings on QGRO. It could be an attractive investment vehicle for moderate- to aggressive-risk-tolerance investors.

QGRO’s Price Target and Stock Market Outlook

As Wall Street expects the S&P 500 to soar 19% to end 2026 around 8300 points, the growth-focused ETFs can outperform the broader stock market indices. Therefore, in 2026, the American Century US Quality Growth ETF share price has the potential to surge nearly 20%. In the past three years, QGRO’s average price return was more than 26%. In 2023 and 2024, its price return was around 32%. In 2021, it delivered its highest annual return of 35%. On the flip side, during a bear market in 2022, the fund’s share price declined 24% compared to a 29% drop for the Russell 1000 Growth Index.

The market fundamentals also support the potential uptrend in 2026. The S&P 500 has already recovered the war-driven losses, with expectations that the first quarter results and solid outlook could fuel a long-lasting uptrend. In particular, the tech stocks are likely to regain investor confidence on the back of their staggering financial growth.

FactSet data indicates the tech sector’s earnings growth forecast is in the range of 45% for the first quarter and 37% for the full year. On the other hand, the US-Iran deal could also enhance investor confidence in equity markets. Furthermore, President Trump’s visit to China in May is also likely to reduce the risk of a trade war between the largest economies.

QGRO’s Unique Strategy Focuses on Risk-Adjusted Returns

The American Century US Quality Growth ETF tracks the performance of the American Century U.S. Quality Growth Index, a rules-based proprietary index. The index uses various metrics in its stock selection process. Initially, the index screens stocks based on their higher profitability, momentum, return on assets, and return on equity. In the second step, it calculates the score of each security based on three factors: sales, earnings, and cash flow growth.

Moreover, in the stock selection process, it also considers valuations through analysis of PE and PB ratios. It ranks stocks based on their overall score. Furthermore, the index also differentiates between high-growth and stable-growth stocks to allocate exposure to each category based on the market environment.

During the periods of bull runs, QGRO boosts its portfolio tilt towards high-growth tech stocks. On the other hand, during bear or challenging conditions, it lifts its stake in stable growers from the industrials, financials, health care, and consumer defensive sectors. The fund rebalances its portfolio on a monthly basis based on the risk-adjusted performance of its holdings. A full portfolio reconstitution occurs on a quarterly basis.

QGRO’s Current Portfolio Seems Well-Balanced

In the past three years, American Century US Quality Growth ETF’s tilt towards the tech stocks was higher. However, so far in 2026, the fund boosted its concentration in stable growers to reduce a volatility factor. The technology sector accounts for 35% of its total weight, down from 45% in the final quarter of 2025.

Similarly, QGRO has also declined its weight in communication and consumer cyclical stocks. Whereas, it lifted its stake in the industrials sector. Moreover, it has also increased its weight in the financials and consumer defensive sectors compared to the final quarter of 2025.

During the recent selloff, these changes helped the quality-focused ETF to limit the downtrend compared to growth ETFs. QGRO’s price declined 12% from its peak to bottom compared to the Russell 1000 Growth Index drop of more than 16%.

In addition, to reduce a risk factor, QGRO limits its stake in each holding. Currently, the fund’s top 10 stocks represent 30% of its total weight. This is down compared to the median of all ETFs of 42%. Russell’s 1000 Growth Index’s top 10 holdings account for 55% of its overall weight. The QGRO portfolio comprises 191 stock holdings.

However, moving forward, the tech stocks upside momentum may compel fund managers to boost their concentration in high-growth stocks. In the previous uptrends, QGRO had lifted its stake in high-growth tech stocks to capitalize on bullish market conditions.

Other Key Characteristics

While American Century US Quality Growth ETF’s dividend yield is lower, its dividend growth rate is worth noting. In the trailing twelve months, QGRO’s dividend growth rate was around 27%. In the last five years, its dividend growth CAGR was close to 19%. The fund’s dividend growth is attributed to its monthly and quarterly portfolio rebalance strategy. The fund distributes realized gains to shareholders through dividends. Excluding 2024 and 2020, QGRO lifted its dividends each year since its inception in 2018.

Contrary to my buy rating, QGRO earned a hold rating based on the Seeking Alpha quant analysis. The fund’s soft price momentum than peers negatively impacted its overall score. Here it is important to note that QGRO seeks to earn risk-adjusted returns rather than chasing high-momentum stocks.

On the positive side, QGRO’s expense ratio of 0.29% is lower than the median of all ETFs of 0.50%. On the other hand, the fund’s liquidity is also strong enough to create an ease in the trading process. In the last three months, its average shares trading volume hovered around 145 thousand, above the median of all ETFs of 42 thousand.

Risk Factors to Consider

While QGRO seeks to limit a risk factor through various techniques, it still carries a higher risk factor compared to the median of all ETFs. In the trailing twelve months, the fund’s standard deviation and price volatility hovered in the range of 12 and 16%. However, these two risk metrics of the Russell 1000 Growth Index were around 15 and 18%, respectively. The fund can respond to tech-driven selloffs and investors rotation to value and defensive stocks. Its performance can also be dented by geopolitical conflicts and trade wars.

In Conclusion

American Century US Quality Growth ETF could be a compelling investment vehicle for the moderate to aggressive risk-tolerance investors to access the growth category. QGRO has the potential to deliver nearly 20% in annual return during bullish market conditions. It can also limit the impact of a downtrend compared to the broader growth indices. The fund’s expense ratio is low, and liquidity is healthy. QGRO’s dividend growth is stronger than other growth-focused ETFs.

data.title: TCAI: Strong Tailwinds, Priced-In Valuations
data.description:
I recently analysed one of the ETFs of Tortoise Capital which led me to the present fund under discussion, the Tortoise AI Infrastructure ETF (TCAI). Although I have analyzed a fair number of ETFs, analyzing Tortoise's fund offers several interesting elements to examine. TCAI is a recently launched fund focusing on AI-related infrastructural investments. The primary objective of the fund is to seek long-term capital appreciation by investing in AI-enabling infrastructure companies, with income generation being a secondary objective. This is again an actively managed ETF, executed by the same four person team as was the case with my earlier-coverage on the Tortoise Energy Fund ETF (TNGY) from same Investment Advisors, Tortoise Capital. The expense ratio of ~0.65% is reasonable considering the active allocation of the fund.

When Everyone is Digging for Gold, Sell Shovels

This phrase precisely defines the investment case, if any, for this fund. While we keep hearing headlines every day and week about new AI players emerging or whopping billions of dollars being raised by AI companies, ever wondered where all of that money goes? There would naturally be the other end of the curve where all that capital flows and that is exactly where the underlying companies in which TCAI is mandated to hold positions operate. The below graph representing Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN), Meta Platforms, Inc. (META), Alphabet Inc. (GOOG), Oracle Corporation (ORCL), and Microsoft Corporation (MSFT) suggests their combined capital expenditures to cross $600 billion annually in the next few years. At the same time, while there is intense competition among leading AI players with excessive valuations, some even relating it to dot-com–era valuations to an extent and cautioning investors about falling for narratives. Kindly note, I affirm the views of those group which are citing caution at this juncture for excessive valuations of tech corporations. I do not think it is in anyway rational at present times to start allocating money into these companies being priced entirely with respect to their growth prospects in a market already likely to intensify further.

However, there are ancillary entities which are crucial for these developments, what we call AI enablers. These are physical assets with contracted cash flows, something investors often relate to toll booths. You need to pay to reach your desired destination. These are not something that a new player would emerge and build overnight and gain market share within a year. This takes time and real money (not VC-style capital raises, but funding backed by real earning power through debt & equity) to build this infrastructure. This includes assets generating electricity, fuels, transmission facilities to move output from producers to end users, among related others.

The rising power demand has been persistent for quite some time and continues to increase rather than consolidating. TCAI terms it as, Electricity is the new oil™. However, before being overly optimistic about this model, certain aspects again need to be addressed. These include the current landscape of AI enablers, the risk of oversupply, and most importantly current valuations, along with some other considerations.

Also, these AI enablers, by virtue of their business models, although might be receiving consistent or stable cash flows, do not possess that asymmetric payoff which is the core of long-term investing. Here you would not ideally find one company which offers the compensation for ten other investments gone wrong; therefore, as an investor you need to be careful while evaluating the prospects you are getting relative to the price you are paying. The element of speculation might be a little lower, but so are the profit payoffs.

Performance and Portfolio

Although TCAI uses the S&P 500 as its performance benchmark, it is clearly understandable that this might not be an adequate benchmark to evaluate the performance of active security selection by portfolio managers, especially over shorter time horizons, as is the case at present. Therefore, I have tried using a few other ETFs focusing on Artificial Intelligence, power infrastructure, data centers, etc., to assess how the category as a whole has performed and relatively how well TCAI has done.

Since the performance history is very short, it would not be suggestive of any meaningful merit so far. To the extent applicable, for the time period since inception, TCAI has performed fairly well on both an absolute and relative basis. The return of ~68% in less than a year is exceptionally strong. On a relative front as well, although other AI enabler funds have performed strongly, the performance of TCAI exceeds them.

However, we should also assess expectations from this level and whether there is any probable chance of mean reversion in the near future. The current performance has been largely due to multiple expansion so far and not structural cash flow growth. While some investors might view the current performance as a positive factor for the fund, with respect to fresh allocation, this same outperformance tends to decrease the forward return expectancy meaningfully for the fund.

The fund presently holds a ~59% exposure to digital infrastructure, followed by a ~27% allocation to energy and the remainder to data centers. Since the holding and sector allocation could change at any time and the ETF is a discretionary active allocator, investors should be cautious about using current allocations as a proxy for evaluating the fund's future return outlook. Ultimately, alpha (if any), will be attributable to the security selection decisions of the portfolio managers rather than a static portfolio snapshot at any particular point in time.

A Comment on Valuation Expansion

The recent gains of the fund are not the result of structural cash flow growth but rather of the multiple expansion with which the market has rerated these entities. Just to get a rough sense of this, we can look at some of the top holdings the fund is currently allocated to and gauge their recent price appreciation. The graph below reflects the price appreciation these entities saw over the last year. Leaving Constellation Energy aside, which is largely an energy infrastructure player, all of the other top holdings, which are part of the digital infrastructure sector, have appreciated significantly. This price appreciation, which has already taken place, tends to significantly disadvantage fresh allocations to these names , whether made directly or indirectly via ETFs.

We can simultaneously check the Price-to-Sales ratio to further reflect and underscore the multiple expansion. The reason for not using a simple P/E comparison, or even EV/EBIT, is that some may argue those metrics are distorted in the current case. Moreover, since it is being suggested that these entities carry seemingly high valuation multiples, P/S , which is a more generous measure can be used to support that assertion. Leaving aside Dell, which is still trading at moderate levels, the increase in the ratio for the other entities is significant.

This is also why a little caution might be warranted on TCAI. Even within the infrastructure landscape, there is a meaningful difference between digital-infrastructure enablers, the ones whose prices have increased the most and other players such as energy names, which remain relatively moderate, or even defensive players such as utilities. Had the fund's allocation been tilted less towards names with elevated expectations and multiples, and more towards value-oriented energy and related sectors, the overall valuation profile would have been rather moderate and forward expectations would still might have been fair. However, that does not appears to be the case at present. This is again not to be used as a proxy for the fund's future positioning, given its actively managed nature, but it is suggestive enough that recent price increases have expanded multiples to a meaningful extent, thereby reducing both forward return expectations and the room for error.

Tailwinds are Present but Valuation Leaves the little room

Considering the analysis so far, the primary conclusion with reference to the investment proposition is that there is little doubt about the significant scope of growth for these AI enablers. The below chart further reflects the amount of CapEx done by technology majors, predominantly for AI-related development, which continues to remain a healthy sign for companies operating in the AI infrastructure development space and ETFs allocating to them. The demand prospects remain suggestive of meaningful expansion.

However, the recent rise in their share prices has meaningfully expanded multiples, and therefore not only is the margin of safety significantly impaired for allocating fresh money now, but medium-to-long-range return expectations stand reduced as well. This is not to suggest that there may not still be certain bottom-up investment propositions in this area that can be profitably utilized by the portfolio managers, but rather that the overall sector tailwinds have largely been priced in generously. Therefore, alpha, if any, would largely depend on the underlying security selection skills of the portfolio managers rather than on sector tailwinds. At the same time, with the influx of many ETFs, hedge funds, and other vehicles being set up to invest in both AI tech and ancillary AI enablers, competition for chasing opportunities has increased significantly. Just to put that assertion into context, Brookfield Asset Management recently launched a $100 billion AI Infrastructure Program (November 2025) There are many more similar launches already announced in some form or the other , and likely many more still to come. Now, contrary to what a lot of investors might reflect on this at first glance and take it as a positive, this influx of capital in turn leads to the bidding up of share prices above fair value due to liquidity chasing them, which further decreases forward return expectations.

Since the fund is relatively new and has appreciated quite meaningfully over a short period, and considering all the developments and optimism currently built into the moment, it would be prudent for investors to wait for a correction that might offer a more compelling entry point than the present level.

Limitations to the Hold Recommendation

First and foremost, with reference to sector tailwinds, the overall aspects are eventually positive for the digital infrastructure enablers. The sheer magnitude of spending by big-tech AI players, along with the significant rise in power demand, has the potential to absorb, to a significant extent, the investments these AI enablers are making, which can also sustain continued investor interest in these names. The reason I have assigned a Hold rating is that forward return expectations look inadequate at current hyped valuations. However, those expectations are for a 10–15-year horizon and not the next year or two, therefore it would not be a surprise if the enthusiasm continues for some time, with business potential expanding and multiples expanding even further; albeit from these higher bases and still delivering investors considerable returns. For that matter, even during the dotcom era there were investors who made money speculating, provided they were able to cash out before the crash; so that possibility remains viable. Obviously, the current proposition does not fit into my threshold of a rational investment, but the probability of near-term gains cannot be excluded altogether.

This could go the other way as well, in the case of competitive intensity causing oversupply, something we saw in clean energy, which, if the market recognises early on, could cause a near-term disappointment as well . Therefore, it is ultimately a delicate proposition for investors, and they should be aware that this is due to the nature of the proposition and the inherent uncertainty involved in making a reasonable assertion of any kind. This eventually needs to be given allowance for and cannot be eliminated by any analysis, however comprehensive or seemingly perfect it might be. That uncertainty remains the norm of investing in these kinds of propositions.

In Conclusion

TCAI stands to benefit from the recent and prospective demand in AI infrastructure, data-centre development, and power and energy demand; however, given the meaningful appreciation over the recent period along with a lot of the prospects already being priced in, the current proposition does not offer an adequate margin of safety and is therefore kept on Hold. Should revenues and earnings start to catch up while multiples remain the same, a revisit might be warranted. The same would also be warranted if we were to see meaningful multiple compression due to a market correction in the near future.

data.title: Crane Company Is Too Lofty For My Liking, Even As Earnings Near
data.description:
Whenever I rate a company a "Sell," I'm making the claim that the stock should underperform the market for the foreseeable future. Well, back in early December of last year, I reaffirmed Crane Company (CR) as a "Sell." The stock's valuation looked lofty even after factoring in the strong operational and financial performance that the business had exhibited. Even though there was the prospect of additional growth thanks to acquisition activities, I believed that the company would underperform the market moving forward.

Since then, I have been wrong. Shares are actually up 5.3% compared to the 1.7% rise that the S&P 500 saw. However, it is worth noting that since I originally rated it a "Sell" back in August of last year, the firm has indeed underperformed the market. Shares are up only 1.3%, which pales in comparison to the 8.2% increase that the S&P 500 enjoyed.

The new data provided by management shows that investors should expect continued growth this year. But even with that factored in, the picture of the business looks mixed at best. Yes, it is likely that revenue, profits, and cash flows will rise throughout 2026. But the fact of the matter is that shares are incredibly pricey, and this justifies, in my view, a bearish outlook. This might seem peculiar considering how the company is priced against other similar enterprises. But even though I do put some weight on relative valuation, absolute valuation is more important to me.

This is not to say that my opinion on the matter cannot be changed. If new data comes in that justifies an upgrade, I will happily do so. I am not, by any means, married to my investment calls. It is interesting to note that, after the market closes on April 27, management will be announcing financial results for the first quarter of the company's 2026 fiscal year. The positive thing here is that there is the expectation that revenue will rise. Adjusted net profits should also increase. But unless something really big and unexpected occurs, I don't see myself upgrading the business at this time.

Shares Have Risen Too High

From a purely operational standpoint, I would say that Crane Company is doing a phenomenal job right now. Take the final quarter of the 2025 fiscal year as an example. During that time, revenue amounted to $581 million. That's a nice increase over the $544.1 million that the business reported a year earlier. According to management, acquisitions added only $1.9 million to the top line. Foreign currency fluctuations ended up being responsible for another $5.7 million. Most of the growth, about $29.3 million in total, came from core sales rising. As revenue increased, profitability rose also. Net income expanded from $81 million to $81.7 million. On an adjusted basis, the rise in profits was from $73.7 million to $89.9 million. Operating cash flow inched higher from $202 million to $205.8 million. Stripping out changes in working capital, we get an increase from $76.9 million to $88.2 million. And finally, EBITDA for the business expanded from $107.9 million to $124 million.

The chart above shows financial results for 2025 compared to 2024. Revenue, profits, and cash flows all grew without exception. This is a testament to the overall quality of the business. And in all likelihood, we should expect further expansion moving forward. Management said, for instance, that the backlog for the business grew from $1.24 billion to $1.44 billion year over year. This is definitely a leading indicator of financial performance.

Before we get too much into what the future holds for the firm, however, I do think a bit of discussion about its operating segments is in order. The company currently has two of them. The larger of these is its Process Flow Technologies segment. Unfortunately, this has been a bit of a laggard as of late. For the final quarter of 2025, as an example, revenue amounted to only $309.4 million. That's up only a hair from the $307.3 million reported the previous year. Had it not been for acquisitions and foreign currency fluctuations, revenue would have actually dropped, with management attributing that to weaker chemical end markets. But the good news is that, even as organic revenue worsened, overall segment profits improved, climbing from $58.4 million to $66 million. But with the backlog for this unit having contracted from $376.4 million to $359.9 million, it will be interesting to see if management can continue growing this part of the business in the near term.

The star of the show has been its Aerospace & Advanced Technologies segment. Year over year, revenue surged 14.7%, climbing from $236.8 million to $271.6 million. The vast majority of this came from core sales growth of $33.9 million year over year. Management mentioned that the firm saw not only higher prices that exceeded the rate of inflation but also that the firm enjoyed higher volumes. I do know that, over the last couple of years now, the aviation industry has been particularly attractive. After the COVID-19 pandemic, investments in aviation surged. And that trend looks set to continue moving forward. The fact that the backlog for the segment expanded from $863.8 million to $1.08 billion is also telling. And with this rise in revenue should come higher profits as well. The chart below shows precisely what I mean here.

When it comes to the 2026 fiscal year, management appears to be rather bullish. They expect, for instance, that revenue will come in at between $2.845 billion and $2.875 billion. However, the vast majority of this will be attributable to multiple acquisitions that the company made. This includes its purchase of Druck, Panametrics, and Reuter-Stokes on January 1, as well as its acquisition of Optek-Danulat on the same day. The latter of these generated about $40 million of revenue in 2025. However, management does expect that core sales growth for the company will come in at a mid-single-digit rate. So at least we aren't seeing organic revenue worsen. Combined, these acquisitions cost about $1.30 billion. And as part of my analysis, I incorporated this purchase when valuing the company using the forward estimates for 2026.

On the bottom line, we can expect adjusted earnings per share of between $6.55 and $6.75. Hitting the midpoint of guidance there would mean adjusted net profits of $392.4 million. If we scale up other profitability metrics similarly, we would get adjusted operating cash flow of $427.3 million and EBITDA of $593.2 million. Using these estimates, as well as the results achieved for 2025, we can see in the chart below how shares of the company are priced at the moment. For context, the 2025 figures obviously exclude the impact of acquisitions, especially since management has not provided detailed estimates as to the impact that they would have on profitability for the business. So I would argue that using the forward estimates for 2026 would be more representative of the picture of the business moving forward. Even with that factored in, however, the stock is anything but cheap. However, compared to other similar firms, shares aren't bad. The chart shows that only one of the five companies I compared it to ended up being cheaper than it is on a price-to-earnings basis. And two of them ended up being cheaper when it came to the other two approaches to valuing it.

Hopefully, I will have the opportunity to revise up my rating on the business before long. After the market closes on April 27, management will be announcing financial results for the first quarter of the company's 2026 fiscal year. Leading up to that point, analysts believe that revenue will total about $672.9 million. That would be up substantially from the $557.6 million that the business reported a year earlier. But again, we are talking about the impact of a couple of sizable acquisitions. Earnings per share from continuing operations should grow from $1.34 to $1.41. This would take net profits up from $78.3 million to $83.2 million. On an adjusted basis, we should get a rise from $1.39 to $1.45. This would mean adjusted net profits climbing from $81.7 million to $85 million. In the table below, you can see certain cash flow metrics for the company for the first quarter of last year. In all likelihood, rising revenue, profits, and adjusted profits should result in these being pushed up too.

Takeaway

The data that's currently available indicates to me that Crane Company is most certainly an intriguing prospect. Unfortunately, even though it might be cheaper than other similar enterprises, it is not cheap enough to justify even a "Neutral" stance as far as I am concerned. Even though management is forecasting attractive growth this year, I feel as though that is priced in. And if I am correct about the economy more broadly, then growth expectations for this year will likely be overly aggressive compared to the reality of the situation. Considering all of this, I still think that rating it a soft "Sell" makes sense here.

data.title: Tesla Q1 Preview: Losing The Robotics Race
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Tesla, Inc. (TSLA) shares are now up over 27% since the last time I wrote on the EV giant last summer. That was on the back of a fairly strong stock market and an AI robotics narrative that in theory could help propel the company to the next level as a next generation industrial giant.

While betting against CEO Elon Musk has been a terrible idea, I am not sure if betting on him at this time is a smart move, either. In the past, as Tesla has hit key growth targets and has been pushed to its limits to scale, Musk was very hands on and focused.

The problem Tesla investors face today is that the EV pioneer now faces an equally demanding SpaceX (SPACE) as the rocket company gears up for an IPO likely this year. Musk’s dual focus worked when both companies were smaller and competition was way less intense. Now, however, I think this distraction is starting to bite.

Unlike the EV market where Tesla was arguably a decade ahead of its peers at one point, Tesla is trying to launch and scale a commercial robots offering in a market they have little edge in.

Given this, I think shares remain a hold. While the EV story has been one with a durable moat, the stock is too reliant on the robotics story heading into Q1 earnings later this week.

Why I’m Doing Follow Up Coverage

To be clear, Tesla shares have been on a tear since I last wrote on them. Since the start of this year, however, shares are down over 12%.

Tesla is still, arguably, the world's leading EV and green energy conglomerate. I think there is a ton of growth potential here.

The problem I have with the company is that I think their robotics story is too rich heading into a critical Q1. AI and automation are becoming an exponentially bigger part of the industrial workspace here in the US. There is a wave of onshoring manufacturing happening. The timing for the market could not be better.

The reason I am doing follow up coverage is because I believe Tesla is not executing here well. They do not have the same moat they have had in prior lines of business. Investors should be concerned about this.

Losing The Robotics Race

On the robotics front, we have to understand where Tesla’s current robotics capabilities are today.

When I talk about Tesla’s robotics capabilities, I don’t mean their stationary robots used on the assembly lines in their plants to make products like their Model 3, I mean their bipedal robots (the Tesla Optimus robot line).

On the surface, the market is huge that this robot could address. The total manufacturing sector’s TAM in 2026 is expected to come in at over $29 trillion. If robots like Optimus could help cut even 10% off the costs of manufacturing globally, you’re looking at a smaller, targeted TAM of over $2 trillion. On paper this seems more than big enough to justify Tesla’s valuation and market cap.

The problem here is we need to look at what actual traction Tesla has on this front. Tesla has only produced and operates ~1,000 of these robots. In many ways, these are still in beta testing and are only really being used internally at Tesla factories.

Let’s compare this to Figure, a dedicated robotics startup. They have already shipped their robots to production and have deployed them at customer locations such as BMW’s factories.

In a lot of ways, Tesla has a long way to go to show Wall Street (and retail investors) that their robotics line is serious and industry leading. In the meantime, there have been serious setbacks in the program and key personnel have left the company.

This key talent is not just leaving Tesla and leaving the robotics industry. One key employee for example, Dar, has left Tesla and now works as the VP of Design at one of their robotics competitors, 1X. 1X actively plans to ship their bipedal robots to customers later this year.

By most accounts, Tesla appears to be behind the market on this front. As I talk more about in the valuation section, I don’t see how the valuation makes sense at this point given their lack of leadership in the robotics space.

Q1 Preview

Heading into Q1 earnings on Wednesday after the bell, expectations are strong on Wall Street. On an EPS side, sell-side analysts expect the company to book $0.36/share in EPS on revenue of $22.34 billion. If Musk’s EV giant can hit the street estimates for EPS, this would represent 32.76% YoY growth. If revenue estimates are matched, this would represent a 15.53% YoY revenue bump. On a revenue and EPS revisions front, both metrics have seen 4 upward revisions over the last 3 months and 9 downward revisions.

Wall Street is signaling that they expect growth, yet their expectations are coming down. To me, this aligns with the fact that shares are down 12% YTD.

To be clear, for a company as big as Tesla, both growth metrics would be impressive. Neither of these bumps in top or bottom line will likely be driven by robotics however.

So, in practice, the stock is being driven by an AI automation narrative. Yet, fundamental earnings performance is still well over a year away from baking in any real automation revenues in my opinion. This is a big mismatch.

On the earnings call, I’ll be watching for any indication that Musk has a concrete strategy for building a real robotics moat.

Valuation

On the surface, there is a known phenomenon where Musk companies get what is known as the “Musk valuation premium” meaning his startups and ventures have higher revenue/earnings multiples than his competition. Tesla is a perfect example of that.

Compared to the consumer discretionary sector, Tesla’s forward P/E of 197.65 is a 1,116.90% premium to the sector median of 16.24. Compared to one of its U.S. peers (General Motors (GM)) the company’s forward P/E of 197.65 is a whopping 3012% premium.

If we look under the surface at actual performance, this premium in their valuation is no longer driven by accelerating EV demand. In fact deliveries were only up 6.2% YoY. This is not the level of growth needed to justify this valuation premium in my opinion.

In essence, I believe that most of this valuation premium compared to the consumer discretionary sector as a whole (and compared to other automakers) comes from the market’s expectations that the robotics division of Tesla will power the next leg of growth.

The issue (as I have mentioned above) is that unlike their EV division (where they were arguably ahead of the rest of the industry for 10+ years) Tesla is entering the robotics market in a slog. This is far more competitive than EVs were in 2010-2012 when they launched their first mass-market vehicle (the Model S).

With this, I don’t think the valuation makes sense at this point. Given I am a hold on the stock, I do not know what the fair value of the stock is.

Bull Thesis

To put my concerns into one sentence: I believe Tesla’s valuation doesn’t make sense given their small (if any) edge in the humanoid robotics space compared to competitors.

I stand by my concerns. Bulls will probably argue that I am downplaying their current data moat.

To review, Tesla plans to win the robotics race with data. Their data can be put into two buckets: human teleoperation movement data, and self driving data from Tesla’s vast network of Tesla EVs on the road.

The bulls for Tesla may or may not recognize that the size of their teleoperation data set is fairly small at this point. But what they can definitely point to is the over 8.4 billion miles of Tesla EV road data that can allegedly be repurposed for its new Optimus robots.

In essence, the bull thesis for Tesla on the robotics side is that this data set and neural network can be repurposed quickly so new Optimus robots can better handle 3D environments and have stronger spatial recognition than their competitors (such as Figure).

This is where I think the bull thesis comes short. While these 8.4 billion miles are incredibly valuable for Tesla EVs that move through dynamic environments (each road will be different than the last), factory environments (especially mass assembly lines) tend to be static. They really don’t change much day to day. That’s the whole point of mass production assembly lines: a constant throughput of production with little variance so the people (and machines) can optimize quickly to it.

My point with this is that Tesla’s EV data set they have fine-tuned their neural networks on has diminishing returns for the factory environment. Many of the biggest problems with modern robots are not about the robots' spatial awareness. The big problems are around the robot’s bipedal propulsion (walking on two legs) and the range of motion of the robot's hands. The EV driving data provides little value here in my opinion.

Takeaway

On the surface Tesla appears to be in a strong position with their Optimus robot line. In theory, Tesla should have the best data, the best team, and the best feedback loops (via Gigafactory) to build a dialed-in offering.

The problem is that this is only in "theory." In reality, only 1,000 robots in deployment is not the same data moat the company has on the autonomous driving side thanks to its Teslas on the road.

In essence, the company is starting from near scratch on one of its most ambitious projects ever.

I think the company’s valuation heading into earnings does not make sense given this. With this, I think shares remain a hold until management (mainly Musk) can present a more concrete plan on how they plan to build a real moat in this space.

data.title: Coursera: A Merger With Udemy Cannot Offset AI Concerns
data.description:
Shares of Coursera (COUR) are trading around the all-time lows, driven by the continued overhang of AI concerns, even as the company announced a large merger in the final days of 2025 with Udemy, to provide some counterweight to this trend.

With the pro forma combination trading at operating asset valuations of just hundreds of millions, at a fraction of sales, while operating with large net cash balances, it is evident that investors are fearful here. Dirt-cheap valuations reflect lower expectations, despite the potential for synergies, as AI concerns are very real, making me take a wait-and-see approach.

Other M&A stories are discussed more extensively at Value In Corporate Events.

A Big Deal

In the week ahead of Christmas, Coursera announced an all-stock merger with Udemy, valuing the equity of the combination at $2.5 billion. CEO Greg Hart called the time a pivotal moment in which AI rapidly redefines the skills required for every job in every industry. Hence, organizations and individuals need flexible platforms to learn.

The deal is designed to create greater value, impact, and choice, combined with leading platform capabilities, accelerated AI innovation, increased reach and market opportunities, and an improved financial profile.

Investors in Udemy will obtain 0.800 shares in Coursera, giving them a combined 41% share of the pro forma share count, with the pro forma equity valuation seen around $2.5 billion.

About The Financials

At first glance, both platforms look quite complementary to each other. Of the two, Udemy is the slightly larger business in terms of revenues, with $796 million in revenues reported, while Coursera generates $740 million in sales. Udemy is relatively strong in enterprises, responsible for about two-thirds of sales, while this is precisely the opposite for Coursera.

Udemy posts EBITDA margins of 12%, or $93 million in dollar terms, while Coursera generates $62 million in EBITDA, for margins equal to 8% of sales. Pro forma EBITDA is seen at $155 million on $1.54 billion in sales, with the combination seeing $115 million in annual cost synergies in the first 24 months post-closing.

About The Exchange Ratio

Shares of Coursera traded around the $8 mark when the deal was announced, as 167 million shares were valued around $1.33 billion, this including a $792 million net cash position. This values operating assets at $538 million.

Based on the 59% ownership on a pro forma basis, I peg the pro forma share count at 283 million shares, implying that investors in Udemy will be granted 116 million shares. The 146 million shares outstanding in Udemy were trading around the $6 mark, including a $358 million net cash position, for a roughly $518 million equity valuation, making it so that both firms are valued at approximately the same dollar amount excluding net cash. This gives investors in Coursera the upper hand, amidst a slightly smaller revenue base but, moreover, lower margin profile.

The pro forma share count stands at 283 million shares, with these shares having fallen to the low $6s here, as the market value stands at $1.7 billion. With a pro forma net cash position of around $1.15 billion here, I see net operating asset valuations around $550 million.

That looks very modest for a business generating pro forma sales of $1.5 billion, EBITDA of around $150 million, with synergies creating a roadmap to increase this to $270 million down the road. That implies that the business is currently valued at just 3-4 times EBITDA, seen down to 2 times if synergies are realised.

Moreover, the balance sheet is strong, revealing huge net cash holdings, including associated interest costs received on cash holdings.

Too Simplistic

The issue with the dirt-cheap valuation mentioned above is the misleading definition of EBITDA, as a $95 million EBITDA number for Udemy for 2025 actually came with a $4 million GAAP operating loss. Coursera reported adjusted EBITDA of $63 million, while GAAP operating losses were reported as high as $77 million.

So in between a pro forma EBITDA number north of $150 million, realistic operating losses are reported at $80 million, largely driven by stock-based compensation expenses. Of course this can easily be paid for, as this is paid with dilution. Moreover, the company holds large net cash holdings on which the company receives net interest income. Meanwhile, the company will benefit from synergies as well, creating a credible road for realistic break-even numbers.

Outside this financial discussion, the biggest risk is that of AI disrupting the business. While the combination reported modest sales growth in 2025, the reality is that AI is rapidly emerging here, being a real threat to the business.

What Now?

Since the merger announcement, shares have fallen from the $8 mark to the $6 mark, which means that the pro forma valuation has fallen by over half a billion, with the implied operating valuations cut in half.

This could be a scare of the market in relation to the continued and relentless rise of AI, the concerns of integrating 2 cultures at an important point, perhaps anticipated integration troubles, and the lack of real profitability. On the other hand, shares trade at very modest valuations here, and even if realistic profits are nonexistent right now, viable synergy estimates could make that happen. On the other hand, AI could make the business more efficient, as management already indicated a big buyback post-closing, without the size being indicated, or the timing of the deal seen closing.

Actually, it was 2021 when I concluded that Coursera provided study material to investors soon after its offering. While investors were blinded by the potential, I feared the losses and poor sentiment across the sector, handsomely awarding the business a >$5 billion valuation at the time at $45 per share at around 15 times sales. Note that the operating asset valuation of the pro forma business now stands at just around 0.3 times sales, suggesting a 50 times compression in multiples on this front!

So, clearly the AI scare is real, amidst a lack of realistic profits to start with, but frankly, expectations are dismal. Shares either are a huge value trap, creating adverse outcomes for investors (although the combination has huge net cash holdings to avoid/prolong this), or they provide a real opportunity given the valuation and potential for synergies.

While I am not advocating a short position, I am hard-pressed to issue an upbeat voice as well, even after the announced deal. Much more hard work remains to be done, as the past does not necessarily instill a lot of confidence here.

data.title: Monster Beverage: Premium Valuation, But 2027 Upside Remains
data.description:
This year, I have expanded my coverage to the beverage industry. I already covered The Coca-Cola Company (KO) and PepsiCo (PEP), and in this report, I am adding Monster Beverage Corporation (MNST) to my coverage. I believe the company has some appeal following the recent share price decline that makes it a buy, but there are some risks as well.

Monster: Less Reliant on Emerging Market and Out-of-Home Sales but With Risks

In my report on The Coca-Cola Company, I already noted that while people will not stop drinking beverages when the economy cools, beverage companies are not insulated from slowing GDP growth. That also somewhat explains why the stock prices of beverage companies have come under pressure since the war in Iran started. For that reason, it is also not a major surprise that we see a company such as Monster trade around 12% lower compared to its pre-war highs.

There are some elements, however, that may make Monster Beverage attractive. The lower share price for a long-term investment may provide an attractive entry point. However, there is more to it than just a lower share price. When economic growth cools, we generally see out-of-home channels cool a bit faster, and Monster is a bit less focused on out-of-home compared to, for instance, Coca-Cola. This could make the stock somewhat more attractive. At the same time, we note that Monster is less reliant on emerging markets, which tend to be sensitive to GDP growth disruptions. Monster derives more of its sales from North America and Europe.

So, beyond my coverage expansion in the beverage industry, Monster Beverage caught my attention for a potential investment for the following reasons:

  1. Lower share price compared to pre-war setting.
  2. Less reliant on out-of-home sales channels.
  3. Less reliance on GDP-sensitive emerging markets.

That, however, does not mean that this makes Monster a buy for the simple reason that there are also risks such as increasing aluminum prices and forex rates along with an overarching demand risk.

Monster Has a Poor Track Record of Satisfying Wall Street Estimates

While it is definitely the case that estimates may soften in the weeks and months ahead, we note that revenue estimates have held up well, with $2.15 billion in sales expected, signaling growth of 16.2%. Over the past month, the downward revision was 0.14%, with +2.4% and +4.2% for the three- and six-month periods. So, those are largely positive revisions for the upcoming quarter. We note that for Q2 and Q3 there has been a 1.1% upward revision. So, we are not seeing major negative revisions at this point.

On earnings per share, 16.5% growth is expected to $0.52, with estimates improving 1.8% over the past six months but down over the past 3 months. We also note that for Q2 and Q3, EPS estimates have declined by 2.8% and 0.5%, but that still signals double-digit year-on-year growth.

The problematic part for Monster is that its track record of beating analyst estimates is not great. On revenues and EPS, the company has missed estimates 7 out of the last 12 quarters, with the slight positive being that the company beat estimates over the last three quarters. With demand and cost risk increasing, the track record of Monster is not really a promising one.

Monster Beverage Initiated With Buy Rating

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and update the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

Monster Beverage trades at a significant premium compared to the peer group, but that is something that I have seen with companies such as Coca-Cola and Pepsi as well. So, I am not too concerned about the lack of upside against a peer group valuation. What we do see is that the stock price currently does not have the 2026 earnings priced in, while typically at this point we would see that 2026 earnings should be priced in fully. So, that adds some appeal, and against expected earnings for 2027, we see 20% upside with a $92.28 price target.

Expected annual sale growth through 2028 is expected to be around 9% with nearly 11% annual growth in EBITDA and 8.8% growth in free cash flow. The free cash flow generation is not exactly what I would like to see since it falls short of EBITDA and sales growth. We also note the lack of a dividend and potential higher cost and demand risk. However, the company also has no bank debt, and that is really strong as it potentially allows the company to repurchase shares when share prices fall.

Margins are expected to continue rising in the years ahead, but again we note the potential for higher costs. The free cash flow generation is expected to be modest this year, and that also drives the apparent low free cash flow growth against sales and EBITDA growth. We note, however, that this is driven by lower capital expenditures last year, with an increase expected this year. I have not modeled in any share repurchases, but we note that, given the company’s net cash position, there is space to repurchase shares, and especially if share prices weaken further, the appeal to leverage the cash position to repurchase shares grows rapidly.

Conclusion: No Dividend but I Like Monster

While there definitely are risks and the free cash flow growth this year does not look strong compared to last year, I do believe that Monster has appeal even without dividends. The reason is that the stock is trading at a discount to its expected earnings this year, and its net cash position does allow the company to buy back shares, and the impact that makes will grow substantially when share prices are depressed. So, while there are some clear risks, I do believe that the shares are a buy, and if share prices contract further to a level below a trading level of 25.8x EV/EBITDA, it would even be a strong buy.

data.title: KeyCorp: Likely Fairly Valued
data.description:
With the help of record-breaking profits in the bank's fee-generating businesses, KeyCorp (KEY) managed to easily exceed analyst expectations for its bottom and top line in the first fiscal quarter. KeyCorp also benefited from an expansion in its loan portfolio and higher net interest income which were driven especially by growth in its commercial loans. While the bank’s deposits dipped slightly on a sequential basis, the bank saw strong net interest income growth in Q1’26, fueled by favorable deposit mix changes, and the NII outlook is positive as well in the short term. KeyCorp, however, is trading above its longer term P/B ratio and shares appear to be fully valued now, in my opinion.

Previous rating

I rated shares of KeyCorp a hold in my last coverage in January, Strong NII, $1.2B Stock Buyback, Not Cheap, because the regional banking enterprise benefited from higher-for-longer interest rates that allowed the lender to grow its net interest income at double-digits. The bank also guided for more than $1.0B in stock buybacks at the time, providing support for its shares.

Offsetting these positive trends in earnings and capital returns were expectations of falling interest rates in 2026. In the fourth-quarter, KeyCorp continued to see strong performance in its core lending business, but KeyCorp trails regional banking rivals in terms of ROE performance. I believe shares have limited upside here in 2026 and I confirm my hold rating following the first-quarter earnings scorecard last week.

NII and fee businesses drive earnings growth

In Q1’26, the regional banking platform reported $0.44 per-share in non-GAAP earnings, beating the consensus estimate by $0.03 per-share. The revenue figure came in at $1.95B, $14.5M above the analyst consensus due to strong growth for both the bank's net interest income as well as capital markets-oriented fee businesses.

The first-quarter was a robust quarter for banks in general, including KeyCorp, as the Federal Reserve took a cautious approach to rate cuts and U.S. economic growth remains strong. While a new war in the Middle East and turmoil surrounding the Strait of Hormuz, a vital oil artery, have weighed on investor sentiment in early March, the lending market overall is in great shape as well.

KeyCorp benefited from robust loan growth momentum, especially in the commercial segment, driving earnings out-performance vs. Wall Street estimates. For the most recent quarter, KeyCorp reported $107.7B in average loans, showing 3.2% year-over-year growth.

The key to the bank's expansion of its loan base in the first-quarter was strong demand for loans from the commercial sector in a resilient economy: KeyCorp's average commercial loans were published at $78.1B, showing 7.9% year-over-year growth. In other words, the bank's commercial loan activity – which focuses primarily on middle market business and specialized industry sectors – was the main engine behind KeyCorp's loan growth in Q1'26.

In terms of net interest income – a core figure for banks that engage in large-scale loan originations – KeyCorp benefited from favorable changes in its deposit mix as well as fixed asset repricing, resulting in an 11% year-over-year increase in its net interest income: for the first fiscal quarter, the lender reported $1.23B in net interest income while its net interest margin expanded 0.29 PP year-over-year.

KeyCorp expects to generate approximately 9% to 10% year-over-year growth in its net interest income (previously 8-10%) which puts the expected NII range up to around $5.1B for FY 2026. This growth will be driven chiefly by expectations of a higher-for-longer federal fund rate environment as well as tailwinds for income from an expanded loan portfolio.

The war in the Middle East has made it more likely that the Federal Reserve will postpone Fed Fund rate cuts in the near term. In March, the Fed maintained stable federal fund rates in a range of 3.50-3.75% which was driven by concerns about accelerating inflation. Last month, inflation indeed soared to 3.3%, the highest level in two years. However, while banks may benefit from a higher-for-longer rate environment short term, in the longer term interest rates are most certainly going to fall... which poses earnings risks for banks in general.

Further, KeyCorp benefited from robust momentum in its fee businesses, especially investment banking and related services. The bank reported total fee income of $723M, showing 8% year-over-year growth.

While KeyCorp’s fee income did not grow as quickly as its net interest income, a more differentiated view shows that investment banking (M&A advisory and debt-related capital markets advisory) as well as trust & investment services were key to the bank’s growth in Q1’26, with both segments expanding their earnings by 13% year-over-year.

Both segments are dependent on strong equity market performance and an appetite for corporations to raise capital, so a strong U.S. economy is key to KeyCorp’s fee income strength for the remainder of 2026.

KeyCorp’s valuation

KeyCorp has seen, like so many other banks, a major revaluation to the upside in the months of March and April... which was driven by subsiding fears about a prolonged conflict between the U.S. and Iran in the Middle East.

Shares of the regional lender are currently trading at a price-to-book ratio of 1.36X which compares against higher P/B ratios for U.S. Bancorp (USB) and Fifth Third Bancorp (FITB): these banks have price-to-book ratios of 1.50X and 1.43X, with the industry group average price-to-book ratio sitting at 1.42X.

In my last review of KeyCorp I calculated a fair value of $18.71 per-share, using the firm's historical P/B ratio of 1.15X at the time. The regional bank's book value as of the end of the first-quarter was $16.13 (+8.3% Y/Y, -0.9% Q/Q), so the application of the same valuation factor leads me to an updated post-Q1 fair value of $18.55 per-share.

While my fair value estimate for KEY is lower than the bank's share price, I am maintaining a hold rating for the regional lender due to uncertainty about the Fed's exact rate cut timeline, the existence of price-supporting stock buybacks (raised to $1.3B in Q1'26, from $1.2B) as well as the underlying strength of the U.S. economy which props up KeyCorp's capital markets business.

Risks with KeyCorp

KeyCorp’s biggest risk is a faster-than-expected timeline for the Federal Reserve to lower federal fund rates which could happen in the context of a deescalating conflict in the Middle East. Rising crude oil prices contributed to the Federal Reserve delaying rate cuts in March. A rapid drop in energy prices, potentially catalyzed by a peace agreement between the U.S. and Iran could potentially accelerate the Fed’s rate cut timeline, and add to growing earnings and NII pressure for the regional bank going forward.

Final thoughts

KeyCorp reported better-than-expected earnings and revenues for its first fiscal quarter, exceeding analyst estimates, due to strong net interest income and fee income tailwinds in its business, especially in investment banking and advisory. While the U.S. economy is performing well right now, despite higher inflation, I see limited upside for KeyCorp in 2026 given that its shares are already trading approximately 21% above the bank's 3-year average P/B ratio. Most importantly, I have reduced my exposure to the banking sector in general in expectation of falling benchmark rates in 2026 which indicates the potential for contracting net interest income margins. As a result, I only maintain a hold rating for KeyCorp, mainly due to the bank's stock buybacks and 4% dividend yield.
